nanoLlama
0102 0304 0506 07 08 0910 1112131415
CPU Only
Lesson 08 / 15

The Transformer
Block

65 minArchitectureResidual Connections

We now have all the pieces: MHA, FFN, RMSNorm. This lesson wires them together into a single transformer block — the repeating unit of Llama. The critical ingredient is the residual connection: the architectural trick that makes deep networks trainable.

The Residual Connection

Without residual connections, gradients in deep networks vanish — they shrink exponentially as they propagate back through dozens of layers, effectively stopping learning in the early layers. The residual connection (also called skip connection) solves this with one line: output = sublayer(x) + x.

This creates a gradient highway: gradients can flow directly back through the addition without passing through the sub-layer. The sub-layer only needs to learn the residual — the difference from the identity — which is a much easier optimization problem.

One Transformer Block — pre-norm Llama architecture
Input x[B, S, 64]
<-- residual copy
↓
RMSNorm(x)normalize first
↓
MultiHeadAttentiontokens mix info
↓
x = x + attn_outRESIDUAL ADD ✓
+ skip connection
↓
RMSNorm(x)normalize again
↓
SwiGLU FFNper-token processing
↓
x = x + ffn_outRESIDUAL ADD ✓
+ skip connection
Pre-norm vs Post-norm

Original "Attention Is All You Need" paper uses post-norm: normalize AFTER the residual add. Llama uses pre-norm: normalize BEFORE the sub-layer. Pre-norm trains more stably, especially for deeper models, because the residual stream stays unnormalized — gradients flow cleanly through additions.

Implementing the Block

transformer_block.py
FINAL CODE
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Assume these are defined (from lessons 06 and 07)
from multi_head_attention import MultiHeadAttention
from swiglu_ffn import SwiGLUFFN
from rmsnorm import RMSNorm

class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    """
    One Llama-style transformer block.
    Structure: pre-norm + MHA + residual, then pre-norm + FFN + residual.
    This block is repeated n_layers times in the full model.
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, n_heads: int, hidden_dim: int):
        super().__init__()
        # Sub-layers
        self.attn     = MultiHeadAttention(d_model, n_heads)
        self.ffn      = SwiGLUFFN(d_model, hidden_dim)
        # Normalization (one before each sub-layer)
        self.norm1    = RMSNorm(d_model)   # before attention
        self.norm2    = RMSNorm(d_model)   # before FFN

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        x: (B, S, d_model) -> (B, S, d_model)

        Pre-norm pattern:
          x = x + Attention(RMSNorm(x))
          x = x + FFN(RMSNorm(x))
        """
        # --- Sub-layer 1: Self-Attention ---
        # Normalize input, run attention, add residual
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))

        # --- Sub-layer 2: Feed-Forward ---
        # Normalize (possibly enriched) x, run FFN, add residual
        x = x + self.ffn(self.norm2(x))

        return x   # (B, S, d_model) -- shape unchanged throughout!

# Test
block = TransformerBlock(d_model=64, n_heads=4, hidden_dim=256)
x     = torch.randn(4, 64, 64)
out   = block(x)
print(out.shape)   # torch.Size([4, 64, 64]) -- still!

params = sum(p.numel() for p in block.parameters())
print(f"Block params: {params:,}")   # 65,664
💡
The two-liner elegance. The entire transformer block logic is two lines: x = x + attn(norm1(x)) and x = x + ffn(norm2(x)). The residual x + is the gradient highway. The norm(...) is the stability guard. Everything else is parameter initialization and bookkeeping.

Why Residuals Enable Deep Networks

residual_gradient_demo.py
INTUITION
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Demonstrate: gradient magnitude with vs without residuals
class DeepNoResidual(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, depth):
        super().__init__()
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([nn.Linear(32, 32) for _ in range(depth)])
    def forward(self, x):
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = torch.tanh(layer(x))   # no residual
        return x

class DeepWithResidual(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, depth):
        super().__init__()
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([nn.Linear(32, 32) for _ in range(depth)])
    def forward(self, x):
        for layer in self.layers:
            x = x + torch.tanh(layer(x))   # residual!
        return x

depth = 12
x     = torch.randn(1, 32, requires_grad=True)

# Without residual
model_no_res = DeepNoResidual(depth)
out1 = model_no_res(x).sum()
out1.backward()
grad_no_res = x.grad.abs().mean().item()

x.grad = None

# With residual
model_res = DeepWithResidual(depth)
out2 = model_res(x).sum()
out2.backward()
grad_res = x.grad.abs().mean().item()

print(f"Gradient (no residual):  {grad_no_res:.6f}")   # tiny, near 0
print(f"Gradient (with residual): {grad_res:.6f}")    # much larger
# With 12 layers: residual gradients are often 100x-1000x larger!
# This is why ResNets (2015) revolutionized deep learning.
✓
Gradient highway math. With a residual x = x + f(x), the gradient is dx_out/dx_in = 1 + df/dx. The 1 is always there — it's the direct path from output to input through the skip connection. Even if df/dx ≈ 0 (vanishing), the gradient is still at least 1. This is the magic.

Stack N Blocks: The Full Transformer Stack

block_stack_test.py
PREVIEW
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Stack 4 transformer blocks — same pattern Llama uses at scale
n_layers   = 4
d_model    = 64
n_heads    = 4
hidden_dim = 256

blocks = nn.ModuleList([
    TransformerBlock(d_model, n_heads, hidden_dim)
    for _ in range(n_layers)
])

x = torch.randn(4, 64, 64)   # (B, S, d_model)

# Pass through all blocks sequentially
for block in blocks:
    x = block(x)

print(x.shape)   # torch.Size([4, 64, 64]) -- same shape after 4 blocks!

# Total transformer stack parameters
params = sum(p.numel() for p in blocks.parameters())
print(f"Stack params: {params:,}")   # 4 * 65,664 = 262,656

# Key: every block sees the same shape [B, S, d_model]
# This is what makes transformers "stackable" to arbitrary depth.
# GPT-4 has ~120 layers. Llama-3-70B has 80 layers. We have 4.

What You Now Know

  • A transformer block = pre-norm + MHA + residual, then pre-norm + FFN + residual
  • Residual connections create gradient highways that prevent vanishing gradients
  • Pre-norm (normalize before sublayer) is more stable than post-norm
  • Each block is shape-preserving: [B, S, D] in, [B, S, D] out
  • Blocks are stackable: n_layers identical blocks in sequence
  • How does the model know token positions? (next lesson — RoPE)
